old_exact: in relevant part: 1. 2. An individual in adult court is guilty of an offense if the individual engages in any combination of three or more sexual acts or sexual contacts with a minor under the age of fifteen years during a period of three or more months. The offense is a class AA felony if the actor was at least twenty-two years of age at the time of the offense. . . . If more than three sexual acts or contacts are alleged, a jury must unanimously agree that any combination of three or more acts or contacts occurred. The jury does not need to unanimously agree which three acts or contacts occurred.
new_exact: in relevant part: 1. An individual in adult court is guilty of an offense if the individual engages in any combination of three or more sexual acts or sexual contacts with a minor under the age of fifteen years during a period of three or more months. The offense is a class AA felony if the actor was at least twenty-two years of age at the time of the offense. . . . 2. If more than three sexual acts or contacts are alleged, a jury must unanimously agree that any combination of three or more acts or contacts occurred. The jury does not need to unanimously agree which three acts or contacts occurred.
source_quote: N.D.C.C. § 12.1-20-03.1, which reads, in relevant part: 1. An individual in adult court is guilty of an offense if the individual engages in any combination of three or more sexual acts or sexual contacts with a minor under the age of fifteen years during a period of three or more months. The offense is a class AA felony if the actor was at least twenty-two years of age at the time of the offense. . . . 2. If more than three sexual acts or contacts are alleged, a jury must unanimously agree that any combination of three or more acts or contacts occurred. The jury does not need to unanimously agree which three acts or contacts occurred.
note: The markdown conflates two separate numbered subsections of a statutory quote. The PDF source clearly shows item 1 ending with 'The offense is a class AA felony...' followed by '...' then item 2 beginning 'If more than three sexual acts...'. The markdown incorrectly runs them together as '1. 2. An individual...' when they should be properly separated. This is an ingestion/transcription defect, not an editorial change.

note: Extra space before the opening quote in the mid-sentence citation. The PDF shows the quote directly after the period with no intervening space. This is an ingestion artifact where a space was incorrectly inserted before the opening quote of the statute citation.
evidence: DB version has ' " Section' while PDF source (pdftotext) shows '"Section' on the following line (OCR line-wrap). The PDFs authoritative layout confirms no space between closing and opening quotes.

source_quote: The Court cannot find by clear and convincing evidence that the conditions and causes of deprivation are likely to continue, or will not be remedied as required by N.D.C.C. 27-20-44(1)(c)(1).
source: PDF OCR ingestion (pdftotext); verified in rendered page image and cross-referenced against correct citations elsewhere in opinion
evidence: The lowercase letter 'l' (U+006C) appears where a digit '1' should be in the statutory citation. This is an OCR error from PDF ingestion. The correct form '27-20-44(1)(c)' appears twice elsewhere in the opinion (¶14 and ¶16 describing the same statute), proving the intended citation format. The structure 27-20-44(1)(c)(1) matches North Dakota statutory citation conventions for N.D.C.C. § 27-20-44, subdivision (1), paragraph (c), clause (1).
note: This is a clear OCR defect where pdftotext rendered the numeral '1' as the letter 'l'. The error appears in a direct quotation from the juvenile court's written order, but the digit error is an ingestion artifact, not the court's own text. The citation is to N.D.C.C. § 27-20-44(1)(c)(1), not (l)(c)(1).

belongs exclusively to the trier of fact . . . we do not reweigh
credibility or resolve conflicts in the evidence.
evidence: The PDF source shows the proper North Dakota Supreme Court ellipsis style with spaced dots (. . .) rather than the compact three-dot form (...) in the database. This appears in a direct quote from Addai v. State within the court's opinion. The spaced ellipsis is the standard ND court formatting per Garner's and ND precedent.
